texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We prove new instances of Halin's end degree conjecture ( HC ) within ZFC . In particular, we prove that there is a proper class of cardinals κ for which Halin's end-degree conjecture holds. This answers two questions posed by Geschke, Kurkofka, Melcher and Pitz in 2023. Furthermore, we comment on the relationship between HC and the Singular Cardinal Hypothesis , deriving consistency strength from failures of the former. We verify that Halin's conjecture fails on finite intervals of successors of singular cardinals in Meremovich's model, this is a new independence result on HC .
texts.text: We prove new instances of Halin's end degree conjecture ( HC ) within ZFC . In particular, we prove that there is a proper class of cardinals κ for which Halin's end-degree conjecture holds. This answers two questions posed by Geschke, Kurkofka, Melcher and Pitz in 2023. Furthermore, we comment on the relationship between HC and the Singular Cardinal Hypothesis , deriving consistency strength from failures of the former. We verify that Halin's conjecture fails on finite intervals of successors of singular cardinals in Meremovich's model, this is a new independence result on HC .

texts.orig: Given an infinite graph G = ( V, E ) a sequence r = { v n | n ∈ ω } ⊆ V is called a ray of the graph G if it is a one-to-one sequence such that { v n , v n +1 } ∈ E for all n ∈ ω . Halin's end degree conjecture concerns configurations of equivalence classes of rays, the equivalence classes are called ends and they are determined by the following relation: If r 0 , r 2 ⊆ V are rays of G and there is a ray r 1 of G such that r 0 ∩ r 1 and r 1 ∩ r 2 are both infinite, then r 0 and r 2 are related. Ends and the degree of an end were both first defined in [6]. The degree of an end ε , denoted by deg( ε ) , is the supremum of the cardinalities of families F ⊆ ε whose elements are mutually disjoint 1 .

texts.orig: Definition 1 (Halin's end degree conjecture) . Let κ be a regular cardinal. We say that Halin's end degree conjecture holds for κ if for every graph G and for every end ε of G with deg( ε ) = κ , there is a pair ( R , P ) such that R ⊆ ε , the elements of R are mutually disjoint, |R| = κ , P is a set of R -independent paths and ( V , E ) , the ( R , P ) -associated graph, is connected.
texts.text: Definition 1 (Halin's end degree conjecture) . Let κ be a regular cardinal. We say that Halin's end degree conjecture holds for κ if for every graph G and for every end ε of G with deg( ε ) = κ , there is a pair ( R , P ) such that R ⊆ ε , the elements of R are mutually disjoint, |R| = κ , P is a set of R -independent paths and ( V , E ) , the ( R , P ) -associated graph, is connected.

texts.orig: Proof. Follows from the proof of [3, Proposition 5.1] replacing respectvely [3, Cor 4.2 and Cor 4.4] by itens (1) and (2) of Lemma 2.1, we include the details for the readers convenience. Let G = ( V, E ) be a graph with an end ε of degree κ . Let R ⊆ ε be a family of pairwise disjoint rays such that |R| = κ . Since κ > ω 1 and cf( κ ) = κ , by the Greedy Lemma, there exists U ⊆ V and C such that | U | = λ < κ , |C| = κ and C is a collection of internally disjoint ( ε , U ) -combs in G with all spines in R . Now consider the corresponding ( λ, κ ) -graph ( U, C , E ) of ( U, C ) . If | U | = ℵ 0 , then, by Combs Lemma, there exists a ray graph contained in ε . If | U | > ℵ 0 , setting λ = | U | we consider two cases: The first case is cf( λ ) = ω , then by (1) of Lemma 2.1 we may find ( U ′ , C ′ , E ′ ) a ( ℵ 0 , κ ) -subgraph of ( U, C , E ) to which we can apply Combs Lemma and obtain a ray graph. The second case is cf( λ ) > ω . In this case, we may apply (2) of Lemma 2.1 and obtain ( U ′′ , C ′′ , E ′′ ) with | U ′′ | < λ a ( λ, κ ) -subgraph of ( U, C , E ) . Iterated applications of (2) of Lemma 2.1 produces a finite decreasing sequence of cardinals which stops when we find a ( ̂ U, ̂ C , ̂ E ) which is a ( ℵ 0 , κ ) -sub-graph of ( U, C , E ) and again Combs Lemma ensures that there exists a ray graph as we sought.

Let r = { v n | n ∈ ω } be a set disjoint from each G α for α ∈ cf( κ ) . We start defining our graph G by adding { v n , v n +1 } to the set of edges of the graph for every n ∈ ω . For each α < cf( κ ) , we fix a ray t α ∈ G α and add the vertices { ( v n , ( t α ) n } to G for every α < cf( κ ) and every n ∈ ω , that concludes our definition of G .
